title: What Is a File?
subtitle: Everything on your computer is a file

sections.content: A **file** is a container for information stored on your computer. Everything you interact with — photos, documents, songs, apps — is a file.
sections.analogy: A file is like a single document in a filing cabinet. It has a name, it contains something (text, an image, music), and it lives somewhere specific.
keyPoints: Everything on your computer is a file: photos, documents, songs, apps
Files have types (extensions): the part after the dot tells the computer what kind of file it is

sections.instruction: Click each file to see its name, type, and what kind of content is inside.

examples.photo.jpg: A beautiful sunset over the ocean — this is an image file
examples.resume.pdf: Sara's professional resume with work experience and skills
examples.notes.txt:
Remember to buy groceries
Pick up dry cleaning
Call dentist
examples.song.mp3: Audio file: 3 minutes 45 seconds of music

sections.question: What type of file is "birthday.jpg"?
sections.explanation: The .jpg extension tells us this is an image file. JPG is one of the most common image formats.

sections.question: If you see a file ending in .html, what does it contain?
sections.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language — it's the code that makes web pages. Every website you visit is built with HTML files.

completionMessage: You now know what files are — containers for information, each with a name and a type. Next, we'll learn how files are organized into folders.
